intro:
Three is the Bible's number of completeness and decisive divine action. Where seven speaks of covenant fullness and twelve of the people of God, three marks what is firm, settled, and especially what is of God — above all the great turning point of resurrection "on the third day."

The pattern appears early and often. God's holiness is praised in a threefold acclamation, "Holy, holy, holy is the LORD of hosts" (Isaiah 6:3), the superlative of perfection. The covenant rests on three patriarchs — the God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob — and Israel is blessed with a threefold benediction (Numbers 6:24–26). Crucially, the "third day" recurs as the day of divine visitation and deliverance: Abraham lifts his eyes and sees Moriah "on the third day" (Genesis 22:4); the LORD descends on Sinai "on the third day" (Exodus 19:11); Jonah is in the belly of the fish "three days and three nights" before being cast out alive (Jonah 1:17); and Hosea hopes, "on the third day he will raise us up" (Hosea 6:2).

These threads converge on Christ. He names Jonah's three days as the sign of his own burial and rising: "so will the Son of Man be three days and three nights in the heart of the earth" (Matthew 12:40). The gospel itself is dated by the number: "he was raised on the third day in accordance with the Scriptures" (1 Corinthians 15:4; Luke 24:46). The pattern of completion through threes marks his ministry too — three temptations overcome, Peter's threefold denial answered by a threefold restoration and commission (John 21:15–17).

Most profoundly, the New Testament reveals the one God in three persons. Jesus commands baptism "in the name of the Father and of the Son and of the Holy Spirit" (Matthew 28:19), and the apostolic blessing invokes all three: "The grace of the Lord Jesus Christ and the love of God and the fellowship of the Holy Spirit" (2 Corinthians 13:14). The number does not by itself prove the doctrine, but the threefold "Holy" of Isaiah is echoed before the throne in heaven (Revelation 4:8), and the whole pattern harmonizes with the Triune life of God. Three thus signals the fullness of God himself and the completeness of his saving work — sealed forever in the third-day resurrection of his Son.
